point_001: The technical novelty is limited: using Google Search for retrieval and static few-shot prompts are not new ideas, and the re-ranking methods build on existing work without sufficient differentiation from prior approaches such as Izacard & Grave 2021 and ART (Sachan et al. 2022).
point_002: The motivation for the three re-ranking methods (RAG-style, noisy-channel, and Product-of-Experts) is poorly explained; each receives only a brief description with no justification for why these specific formulations were chosen or how they differ conceptually.
point_003: The paper does not adequately discuss the large gap between its results and state-of-the-art performance (e.g., 38.4 vs. 55.9 on NQ, 30.3 vs. 65.2 on HotpotQA), nor does it suggest how this gap might be closed.
point_004: Experiments are conducted almost exclusively on the Gopher model family; testing on other large language models (e.g., GPT-3, OPT) would strengthen generalizability claims.
point_005: Reproducibility is severely limited because Gopher is not publicly available, and very few institutions have access to 280B-parameter models.
point_006: The paper does not compare against more recent and stronger retrievers (e.g., Contriever) in Table 1, making it hard to assess whether improvements stem from Google Search quality or from the proposed method itself.
point_007: It is unclear whether performance gains come from the retrieval-augmented approach or simply from the high quality of Google Search results; ablations using other retrieval sources (e.g., Wikipedia, the model's pretraining corpus) are needed.
point_008: The effect of the number of few-shot examples (k) on performance is not studied, and no ablation explores whether simply increasing k could yield comparable improvements.
point_009: A natural baseline of concatenating multiple retrieved paragraphs into a single context for joint inference is not evaluated, making it unclear whether the multi-answer generation and re-ranking pipeline is necessary.
point_010: The paper does not explore dynamic or query-adaptive prompting strategies (e.g., retrieving similar questions as in-context examples), which could be more practical and potentially more effective than fixed, dataset-specific prompts.
point_011: Evaluation is limited to four datasets; broader evaluation on knowledge-intensive benchmarks such as KILT would better demonstrate the method's scope.
point_012: The relationship between the proposed approach and closely related concurrent work on few-shot retrieval-augmented language models (e.g., Izacard et al. 2022) is not clearly articulated.
point_013: The abbreviation 'LSLM' is introduced without being defined, and a paragraph in Section A.4 appears misplaced, indicating minor but notable clarity issues.
